title: Resource Identity
description: The canonical, four-plane domain model describing the complete identity of a resource. This is the internal model produced by the Convention Engine; it is not the public request contract (see naming-request.schema.json).

organizationalIdentity.description: Plane 1: Organizational Identity. Answers 'Why does this resource exist?'. These attributes are highly stable and rarely change over the lifetime of a resource.

organization.description: Enterprise, company, legal entity, or top-level owner of the resource.

business_unit.description: Organizational area responsible for or funding the system.

system.description: Software system, product, or business application to which the resource belongs.

tenant.description: Optional customer or logical tenant associated with the resource.

deploymentIdentity.description: Plane 2: Deployment Identity. Answers 'Where is this resource deployed?'.

platform.description: The platform the resource is deployed to (for example, a cloud provider or orchestration platform).

deployment_scope.description: The platform-independent isolation boundary the resource is deployed into (for example, an AWS Account, an Azure Subscription, or a Kubernetes Cluster).

environment.description: The environment the resource is deployed to (for example, production or staging).

location.description: The physical or logical location of the deployment (for example, a region or datacenter).

instance.description: Distinguishes multiple instances of an otherwise identical resource.

functionalIdentity.description: Plane 3: Functional Identity. Answers 'What does this resource do?'. Follows Domain-Driven Design concepts.

domain.description: The bounded context or business domain the resource belongs to.

service.description: The service within the domain that the resource supports.

component.description: The specific building block or module within the service.

resource_type.description: The type of the resource being identified.

operationalMetadata.description: Plane 4: Operational Metadata. Answers 'Who owns, supports and governs the resource?'. This plane normally becomes tags, labels, or annotations, and should not usually participate in resource names.

owner.description: The individual or team accountable for the resource.

cost_center.description: The cost center the resource should be billed to.

criticality.description: The operational criticality of the resource.

classification.description: The data or security classification of the resource.

managed_by.description: The tool, team, or process responsible for managing the resource.
